mnemonics.title: FDM - Frequency Divides Many
mnemonics.content: F requency D ivision M ultiplexing: Think of different radio stations, each broadcasting on its own frequency band simultaneously, like 98.7 FM, 102.5 FM - they don't interfere because they're on different frequencies!

mnemonics.title: TDM - Time Does Matter
mnemonics.content: T ime D ivision M ultiplexing: Imagine a round-robin system where different people speak for exactly 2 seconds each in a queue. Everyone gets their turn, just not all at once!

mnemonics.title: WDM - Wavelength Divides Messages
mnemonics.content: W avelength D ivision M ultiplexing: Picture a prism splitting white light into rainbow colors - each color (wavelength) carries different data through fiber optic cables, all at the same time!

mnemonics.title: The Multiplexing Trio
mnemonics.content: Remember: FDM uses 'F'requency (like radio), TDM uses 'T'ime (like talking turns), and WDM uses 'W'avelength (like light colors). First letter tells you what gets divided!

mnemonics.title: Analog vs Digital Memory
mnemonics.content: FDM = Analog (continuous waves like radio), TDM = Digital (discrete time slots), WDM = Light-based (fiber optics). F and T are older, W is the modern wizard!

acronyms.usage: Use when multiple analog signals need to share a single communication channel by dividing the frequency spectrum. Example: Radio broadcasting, TV channels
Use when digital signals need to share a channel by assigning specific time slots. Example: Telephone networks, digital audio broadcasting
Use in fiber optic communications to combine multiple light signals of different wavelengths onto a single fiber. Example: High-speed internet backbone
Use when time slots need to be dynamically allocated based on demand (more efficient than synchronous TDM). Example: Modern data networks

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a highway with each car having its own dedicated lane (frequency band). All cars travel at the same time but in different lanes - they never collide! Draw horizontal parallel lines, each at different 'height' representing frequency.
Picture a traffic light cycle where Lane 1 gets 10 seconds, then Lane 2 gets 10 seconds, then Lane 3 gets 10 seconds, then repeats. Draw a timeline with slots: [A][B][C][A][B][C]...

Think of a rainbow spectrum. Imagine red light carries data 1, orange light carries data 2, yellow carries data 3, and so on - all traveling together through one glass fiber! Draw multiple colored lines (wavelengths) stacked vertically but traveling horizontally.
Draw a funnel shape (MUX) combining multiple inputs into one output, then an inverted funnel (DEMUX) splitting one input back into multiple outputs - like a combining and separating pipe system!

analogies.analogy: Imagine a concert hall with 5 different acoustic zones. Each zone has its own speaker playing different music simultaneously. People in each zone hear only their music because the sound frequencies are separated! This is FDM - different frequency bands, same medium, no interference.

Think of 4 friends sharing one phone to wish their 4 other friends birthday wishes. They take turns: Friend A speaks for 3 seconds, then Friend B speaks for 3 seconds, then C, then D, then back to A. Each gets equal time, no one talks over another!

Imagine shining 10 different colored laser pointers into one glass tube. Each color (wavelength) carries different information. They all travel together without mixing, then a prism at the end separates them again. Like optical magic!

Think of a busy restaurant kitchen. Multiple orders come in ( appetizers, mains, desserts from different tables), the head chef (MUX) combines them into one coordinated delivery schedule, then the server (DEMUX) delivers each dish to the correct table!

Imagine a pizza (total bandwidth). In FDM, you cut the pizza into 6 slices (frequency portions) and give each person a whole slice. In TDM, you give each person the whole pizza for 2 minutes, then rotate. Both get pizza, just different sharing methods!
